<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="utf-8" />
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" />
<title>Étale cohomology — covers, ℓ-adic Betti, and the Weil conjectures</title>
<link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.css">
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/katex.min.js"></script>
<script defer src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/katex@0.16.11/dist/contrib/auto-render.min.js"></script>
<!-- breadcrumb-head-auto-begin -->

<script src="./js/breadcrumb.js"></script>
<script src="./js/glossary-popover.js" defer></script>
<link rel="stylesheet" media="print" href="./css/print.css">
<link rel="stylesheet" href="./css/theme-light.css">
<script src="./js/theme-toggle.js"></script>
<script src="./concepts/bundle.js" defer></script>
<script src="./js/topic-hotkeys.js" defer></script>
<script src="./js/topic-lineage.js" defer></script>
<!-- breadcrumb-head-auto-end -->
<script src="./js/progress.js"></script>
<script src="./js/quiz.js"></script>
<script src="./js/katex-select.js"></script>
<script src="./quizzes/bundle.js"></script>
<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      throwOnError:false,
      macros:{
        '\\Spec':'\\Spec',
        '\\Gal':'\\Gal',
        '\\Hom':'\\Hom',
        '\\tr':'\\tr',
        '\\ad':'\\ad',
        '\\ind':'\\ind'
      }
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%} svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none} nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0} aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)} aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem} .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  /* callback-css-auto-begin */
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
/* callback-css-auto-end */
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-begin -->
<script src="./js/display-prefs.js"></script>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-end -->
</head>

1 Étale morphisms 2 ℓ-adic cohomology 3 Lefschetz trace 4 Weil & Frobenius 5 Comparison theorems

Étale cohomology, ℓ-adic Betti, and the Weil conjectures

Grothendieck's replacement for singular cohomology in characteristic $p$. Covers of schemes that behave like local homeomorphisms, a cohomology theory that counts the same Betti numbers as topology, and a Frobenius whose trace counts $\mathbb{F}_q$-points.

blocks.html:
1. Étale morphisms

Over $\mathbb{C}$ a map of complex manifolds is a local homeomorphism iff its differential is invertible everywhere. Over a scheme the condition splits into three algebraic pieces: flat (fibres vary continuously), unramified (each fibre is a product of separable field extensions of the base point), and locally of finite presentation. The conjunction is étale.

Concretely, $f\colon X\to Y$ is étale iff every point has an affine patch $\Spec B \to \Spec A$ with $B = A[t_1,\dots,t_n]/(g_1,\dots,g_n)$ and Jacobian $\det(\partial g_i/\partial t_j)$ a unit in $B$ — a "complete intersection with non-vanishing Jacobian."

The warning bell in char $p$ is Frobenius $F\colon t\mapsto t^p$: its differential $pt^{p-1}$ vanishes, so $F$ is finite, flat, bijective on points, yet ramified everywhere. Étale cohomology refuses to count it as a cover.

On $\mathbb{G}_m = \Spec k[t,t^{-1}]$ the Kummer map $t\mapsto t^n$ is étale exactly when $n$ is invertible in $k$ — equivalently $\gcd(n,p)=1$ in char $p$, always in char $0$. When étale, the fibre over $t$ is the finite étale scheme of $n$-th roots of $t$ with Galois group $\mu_n$.

Étale morphisms are the "right" notion of cover: Zariski is too coarse, topological covers of $X(\mathbb{C})$ don't exist in char $p$, and étale splits the difference — algebraic, and recovering ordinary covers of $X(\mathbb{C})$ over $\mathbb{C}$.

params.title: Étale cover explorer: $t\mapsto t^n$ on $\mathbb{G}_m$

layout.html: n = 3

params.title: Étale cover explorer: $t\mapsto t^n$ on $\mathbb{G}_m$

layout.html: n = 3

blocks.html:
Étale site. Étale morphisms $U\to X$ with jointly-surjective families as covers form a Grothendieck site; sheaves on it are the primary objects. The étale fundamental group $\pi_1^{\text{ét}}(X,\bar x)$ classifies finite étale covers and unifies $\Gal(\bar k/k) = \pi_1^{\text{ét}}(\Spec k)$ with the profinite completion of the topological $\pi_1$ of $X(\mathbb{C})$.

A quick local test is Jacobian invertibility. For $f(t)=t^n-u$, étale over a point means $f'(t)=nt^{n-1}$ stays invertible on the fiber — exactly where inseparability disappears.

params.title: Jacobian unit test

2. ℓ-adic cohomology

A first naive try — $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z})$ defined by mimicking singular cohomology on the étale site — fails: constant $\mathbb{Z}$-coefficients give vanishing higher cohomology on most $X$. Torsion is where the action is.

Fix a prime $\ell\neq p$. The constant sheaf $\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n$ has a good étale cohomology $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)$; these finite groups are compatible as $n$ grows. Take the inverse limit and tensor with $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$:

$$H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) \;:=\; \Big(\varprojlim_n H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n)\Big)\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}_\ell}\mathbb{Q}_\ell.$$

For $X$ smooth proper this is a finite-dimensional $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$-vector space with the expected formalism: Künneth, Poincaré duality, a Lefschetz trace formula, a cycle class map. The restriction $\ell\neq p$ is essential — $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/p^n)$ sees Frobenius-kernel pathology and is handled by crystalline cohomology instead.

Comparison theorem. If $X$ is a smooth projective variety over $\mathbb{C}$, then $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)\cong H^i_{\text{sing}}(X(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Q})\otimes\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ for every $\ell$. The $\ell$-adic Betti numbers $b_i = \dim_{\mathbb{Q}_\ell}H^i_{\text{ét}}$ agree with the classical ones.

For $X$ smooth projective of dimension $d$, some Betti numbers are automatic: $b_0 = b_{2d} = 1$, $b_1 = 2g$ for a curve of genus $g$, $b_{2i}\ge 1$ from the $i$-fold hyperplane class. The Euler characteristic $\chi(X) = \sum(-1)^i b_i$ is $2-2g$ for curves, $c_2$ for surfaces, $n+1$ for $\mathbb{P}^n$.

blocks.html:
Sanity check: a smooth cubic surface has $b_2 = 27-36+18-2 = 7$ and $\chi = 9$ — matching the classical Picard rank (27 lines, rank 7). $E\times E$ has Künneth Betti numbers $(1,4,6,4,1)$ and $\chi = 0$, as expected for an abelian surface.

Inverse limits can feel abstract. The next panel truncates at level $n$ and shows stabilization: once $n$ is large enough, low-precision information has essentially settled, and tensoring with $\mathbb{Q}_\ell$ removes torsion noise.

Étale cohomology · Grothendieck–Lefschetz fixed-point formula

3. Grothendieck–Lefschetz fixed-point formula

The classical Lefschetz fixed-point theorem says: for a continuous self-map $f\colon X\to X$ of a nice compact space whose graph meets the diagonal transversally, the signed count of fixed points equals the alternating trace of $f$ on cohomology,

$$\#\mathrm{Fix}(f) \;=\; \sum_i (-1)^i \,\mathrm{Tr}\big(f^*\,\big|\,H^i(X,\mathbb{Q})\big).$$

Grothendieck translates this to the étale setting. For $X$ smooth proper over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_q}$ and an endomorphism $f\colon X\to X$ with graph $\Gamma_f$ transverse to the diagonal $\Delta\subset X\times X$, the intersection number $\Gamma_f\cdot\Delta$ can be computed by pulling back the class of $\Delta$ through $f$ on $H^*_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)$. The algebraic version reads

$$(\Gamma_f\cdot\Delta)_{X\times X} \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{2\dim X}(-1)^i\,\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(f^*\,\big|\,H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell)\big).$$

Apply this to the geometric $q$-power Frobenius $F_q$. A fixed point of $F_q^{\,m}$ on $X(\overline{\mathbb{F}_q})$ is exactly an $\mathbb{F}_{q^m}$-rational point, and transversality holds automatically (the derivative of Frobenius is zero, so graph and diagonal meet cleanly). Out falls the identity driving the Weil conjectures:

$$\#X(\mathbb{F}_{q^m}) \;=\; \sum_{i=0}^{2\dim X}(-1)^i\,\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(F_q^{\,m}\,\big|\,H^i_{\text{ét}}\big).$$

params.title: Trace formula sanity check

layout.html: n = 2 g = 1 a_q = 1 m = 2

params.title: Trace formula sanity check

layout.html: n = 2 g = 1 a_q = 1 m = 2

blocks.html:
Why transversality is free for Frobenius. On a smooth variety over $\overline{\mathbb{F}_q}$, the differential $dF_q$ is identically zero on tangent spaces. So $T_x(\Gamma_{F_q})\oplus T_x(\Delta) = T_x(X\times X)$ at every fixed point — every intersection is transverse, every fixed point contributes $+1$, and the formula gives the honest count $\#X(\mathbb{F}_{q^m})$ with no correction terms.

The same machinery reproduces many classical theorems. On $\mathbb{P}^n$ every odd cohomology vanishes and $F_q$ acts as $q^i$ on $H^{2i}$, so $\#\mathbb{P}^n(\mathbb{F}_{q^m}) = 1+q^m+q^{2m}+\dots+q^{nm}$. On a curve of genus $g$ the formula collapses to $\#X(\mathbb{F}_{q^m}) = 1+q^m - \sum_j(\alpha_j^m+\bar\alpha_j^m)$, and the Weil bound $|\,\#X(\mathbb{F}_q)-(q+1)\,|\le 2g\sqrt q$ is immediate from $|\alpha_j|=\sqrt q$.

The cleanest demonstration puts Betti numbers, Frobenius eigenvalues, and brute-force point counts side by side — the trace formula should hold term-by-term. Below, for each variety in the shortlist we display $b_i$, assemble the $H^i$-factor $\mathrm{Tr}(F_q^m|H^i)$ from the recorded eigenvalues, and (where feasible) compare to the actual $\#X(\mathbb{F}_{q^m})$ obtained by enumerating points.

Étale cohomology · Weil conjectures and Frobenius trace

4. Weil conjectures and the Frobenius trace

With the trace formula from §3 in hand, the payoff is the zeta function. Packaging point counts over all $m$ gives $Z(X,t) = \exp\big(\sum_m \#X(\mathbb{F}_{q^m})\,t^m/m\big)$; the Lefschetz trace plus the identity $\exp(-\operatorname{Tr}\log(1-tA)) = \det(1-tA)^{-1}$ yields

$$Z(X,t) \;=\; \prod_{i=0}^{2\dim X}\det\!\big(1 - t\,\operatorname{Frob}_q\,\big|\,H^i_{\text{ét}}\big)^{(-1)^{i+1}}.$$

Three striking consequences are the Weil conjectures, proved by a team spanning Dwork, Grothendieck, and Deligne:

W1 — Rationality. $Z(X,t)\in\mathbb{Q}(t)$. Each factor $\det(1-t\,\operatorname{Frob}|H^i)$ is a polynomial with $\mathbb{Z}$-coefficients of degree $b_i$.
W2 — Functional equation. Poincaré duality forces $Z(X, 1/(q^d t)) = \pm q^{d\chi/2} t^{\chi} Z(X,t)$, with $d = \dim X$ and $\chi = \sum(-1)^i b_i$.
W3 — Riemann hypothesis (Deligne). Every eigenvalue $\alpha$ of $\operatorname{Frob}_q$ on $H^i_{\text{ét}}$ is an algebraic integer with $|\iota(\alpha)| = q^{i/2}$ for every complex embedding $\iota$.

For a smooth curve of genus $g$ with $b_0=b_2=1$, $b_1=2g$: $Z(X,t) = L(t)/((1-t)(1-qt))$, $L(t) = \prod_j (1-\alpha_j t)(1-\bar\alpha_j t)$, $|\alpha_j|=\sqrt q$. For $g=1$, $L(t) = 1 - a_q t + q t^2$, $a_q = \alpha+\beta$, $\alpha\beta = q$, Hasse $|a_q|\le 2\sqrt q$; the eigenvalues sit at $\pm\theta$ on the circle of radius $\sqrt q$.

params.title: Zeta-function factoring for $E/\mathbb{F}_q$

layout.html: a_q = 1

params.title: Zeta-function factoring for $E/\mathbb{F}_q$

layout.html: a_q = 1

blocks.html:
The upshot. Point-counts over $\mathbb{F}_{q^m}$ for all $m$ are packaged into finitely many Frobenius eigenvalues, organized by cohomological degree. Étale cohomology is precisely what makes this packaging exist as a theorem, not an analogy.

Deligne's bound $|\alpha| = q^{i/2}$ is a statement about geometry of complex numbers: after rescaling $\alpha \mapsto \alpha/\sqrt q$ on $H^1$, eigenvalues must lie on the unit circle. Sweep a family of elliptic curves and the two eigenvalues $\alpha_p(t), \bar\alpha_p(t)$ trace out complex-conjugate arcs. As $p$ varies (or as we range over a parameter $t$ in a family), the angles $\theta = \arg(\alpha/\sqrt q)$ distribute according to a measure — for a non-CM elliptic curve, Sato–Tate predicts $\tfrac2\pi\sin^2\theta\,d\theta$.

params.title: Frobenius eigenvalue sweep on the Riemann sphere

layout.html: a_q = 1

params.title: Frobenius eigenvalue sweep on the Riemann sphere

layout.html: a_q = 1

blocks.html:
5. Comparison theorems: étale, singular, de Rham, crystalline

Étale cohomology earns its keep only if it reproduces what we already know when we already know it — and plugs the gap smoothly when we don't. The comparison theorems are the bridge.

Artin's comparison (Artin, Grothendieck, SGA 4). For $X$ a smooth projective variety over $\mathbb{C}$ and any prime $\ell$,

$$H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n) \;\cong\; H^i_{\text{sing}}(X(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Z}/\ell^n), \qquad H^i_{\text{ét}}(X,\mathbb{Q}_\ell) \;\cong\; H^i_{\text{sing}}(X(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{Q})\otimes\mathbb{Q}_\ell.$$

So the $\ell$-adic Betti numbers agree with the classical topological Betti numbers — the same integers, for every $\ell$, computed from totally different data. That independence from $\ell$ is itself a nontrivial consequence.

de Rham comparison (Grothendieck). Over a field $k$ of characteristic zero, smooth varieties carry algebraic de Rham cohomology $H^i_{\text{dR}}(X/k)$ computed from the Koszul complex of Kähler differentials. For $k=\mathbb{C}$,

$$H^i_{\text{dR}}(X/\mathbb{C}) \;\cong\; H^i_{\text{sing}}(X(\mathbb{C}),\mathbb{C}),$$

so étale (after tensoring with $\mathbb{C}$ via a chosen embedding $\mathbb{Q}_\ell\hookrightarrow\mathbb{C}$), singular, and de Rham all agree in char $0$. Hodge theory refines the singular side with the $(p,q)$-decomposition.

Crystalline & p-adic Hodge. When the ground field has characteristic $p$ and we try $\ell=p$, étale cohomology breaks down. Crystalline cohomology (Berthelot, Grothendieck) $H^i_{\text{cris}}(X/W)$ is a module over the Witt vectors $W(k)$ with a semi-linear Frobenius — the right $p$-adic Betti theory. In mixed characteristic, p-adic Hodge theory (Fontaine, Faltings, Tsuji, Scholze) supplies a family of period rings $B_{\text{dR}}, B_{\text{cris}}, B_{\text{st}}$ that relate $H^i_{\text{ét}}(X_{\bar K},\mathbb{Q}_p)$ to $H^i_{\text{dR}}(X/K)$ and $H^i_{\text{cris}}$ after tensoring:

$$H^i_{\text{ét}}(X_{\bar K},\mathbb{Q}_p)\otimes_{\mathbb{Q}_p}B_{\text{cris}} \;\cong\; H^i_{\text{cris}}(X_k/W)\otimes_{W}B_{\text{cris}}.$$

params.title: Pick a geometry, watch the cohomologies line up
params.hint: base field, characteristic, and which theories are available

params.title: Pick a geometry, watch the cohomologies line up
params.hint: base field, characteristic, and which theories are available

blocks.html: Motivic shadow. The fact that $\ell$-adic Betti numbers are the same integer $b_i$ for every $\ell$, that this integer also equals $\dim_{\mathbb{C}}H^i_{\text{dR}}$ and $\dim_{\mathbb{Q}}H^i_{\text{sing}}$, and that Frobenius eigenvalues and their characteristic polynomials are also $\ell$-independent, is evidence for a conjectural motivic cohomology sitting above all these Weil cohomologies — one realization functor for each.

See also
  • Singular homology
  • Stokes theorem and de Rham cohomology
